expanded_description: A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel. This simple definition generates a cascade of properties: opposite sides are congruent, opposite angles are congruent, consecutive angles are supplementary, and the diagonals bisect each other. These properties make parallelograms one of the most useful shapes in geometry, with applications in construction, design, and coordinate proofs.

core_concept.principle: A parallelogram is defined as a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel. From this definition, four key properties follow: (1) Opposite sides are congruent. (2) Opposite angles are congruent. (3) Consecutive angles are supplementary (sum to 180°). (4) The diagonals bisect each other.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a parallelogram as a rectangle pushed sideways—like a deck of cards pushed at an angle. The top and bottom stay parallel and equal in length. The left and right sides stay parallel and equal in length. Opposite angles remain equal. Adjacent angles are supplementary because parallel lines create same-side interior angles. The diagonals always cross at their exact midpoints—this is the property that makes parallelograms unique among quadrilaterals. These four properties are so tightly linked that proving any one of them can establish all the others.

image.alt: A parallelogram with opposite sides marked parallel and congruent. Opposite angles marked with matching arcs. Consecutive angles marked supplementary. Diagonals intersecting with bisection marks.
image.caption: Parallelogram properties: opposite sides ≅, opposite angles ≅, consecutive angles supplementary, diagonals bisect.

sections.introduction: A parallelogram is the most fundamental type of quadrilateral after the trapezoid. Its defining characteristic—both pairs of opposite sides parallel—automatically creates four powerful properties used extensively in proofs and problem-solving.

introduction_image.alt: A parallelogram with opposite sides marked with parallel arrows. Definition stated.
introduction_image.caption: Parallelogram: a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel.

key_concept.definition: A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel: $\overline{AB} \parallel \overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD} \parallel \overline{BC}$. Four theorems follow: (1) Opposite sides are congruent ($\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$, $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{BC}$). (2) Opposite angles are congruent ($\angle A \cong \angle C$, $\angle B \cong \angle D$). (3) Consecutive angles are supplementary ($m\angle A + m\angle B = 180°$, etc.). (4) Diagonals bisect each other—if diagonals intersect at $E$, then $\overline{AE} \cong \overline{EC}$ and $\overline{BE} \cong \overline{ED}$.
key_concept.context: These theorems are proved using parallel line angle relationships and congruent triangles. Drawing a diagonal creates two congruent triangles by ASA, giving opposite sides and angles congruent by CPCTC. Consecutive angles are supplementary because parallel lines make same-side interior angles supplementary. Diagonal bisection is proved by ASA congruence of the two triangles formed by the diagonals.

image.alt: Parallelogram with all four properties illustrated and labeled. Proof diagram showing diagonal creating congruent triangles.
image.caption: Four properties follow from the parallel definition, proved via triangle congruence.

process_flow.description: Confirm the quadrilateral is a parallelogram—given, marked with parallel arrows, or previously proven.
process_flow.mechanism: Look for the word 'parallelogram' in the given or parallel arrow marks on both pairs of opposite sides.

image.alt: Several quadrilaterals with parallel markings. Checkmark confirming parallelogram status.
image.caption: Step 1: Verify the quadrilateral is a parallelogram.

process_flow.description: Use the appropriate theorem for side lengths, angle measures, or diagonal relationships.
process_flow.mechanism: For sides: opposite sides congruent. For angles: opposite angles congruent, consecutive angles sum to 180°. For diagonals: they bisect each other.

image.alt: Parallelogram with property-specific diagrams: sides equated, angles related, diagonals with midpoint marks.
image.caption: Step 2: Apply the appropriate parallelogram property.

classifications.value: The four properties of a parallelogram each solve different problem types.
characteristics: Sides: Opposite sides are congruent. Used to set up equations for unknown lengths.
Angles: Opposite angles congruent; consecutive angles supplementary. Used to find missing angle measures.
Diagonals: Bisect each other. The intersection point is the midpoint of both diagonals.
All four properties are biconditional—they can be used to prove a quadrilateral is a parallelogram.

image.alt: Parallelogram with diagonals intersecting at E. Midpoint marks on both diagonals. Equations shown.
image.caption: Diagonals bisect each other: AE = EC, BE = ED.

image.alt: Complete section overview with the four properties, proofs, and application examples.
image.caption: Properties of Parallelograms overview.

sections.introduction: The parallelogram properties are proved using triangle congruence. These proofs are models for the deductive structure of geometry.

introduction_image.alt: Parallelogram with diagonal drawn, creating two congruent triangles. ASA proof shown.
introduction_image.caption: Proving parallelogram properties via triangle congruence.

worked_examples.title: Parallelogram Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) In parallelogram ABCD, AB = 2x+3, CD = 5x−9. Find x and AB.
2) In parallelogram PQRS, m∠P = 65°. Find ∠Q, ∠R, ∠S.
3) Diagonals intersect at E. AE = 4x−3, EC = 2x+7. Find AC.
4) Prove: Opposite sides of a parallelogram are congruent.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw diagonal $\overline{AC}$. Alternate interior angles from parallel lines give $\angle BAC \cong \angle DCA$ and $\angle BCA \cong \angle DAC$. $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{AC}$. $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle CDA$ by ASA. $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$ and $\overline{BC} \cong \overline{DA}$ by CPCTC.

image.alt: Four panels: side equation, angle measures, diagonal bisection, congruence proof.
image.caption: Parallelogram problems: side, angle, diagonal, and proof.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Parallelogram properties for sides, angles, and diagonals.

sections.introduction: Practice applying the four parallelogram properties to find missing values and prove relationships.

worked_examples.title: Parallelogram Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) m∠A = 72°. Find all angles.
2) WX = 3x−4, YZ = x+10. Find WX.
3) BE = 2x+1, ED = 3x−6. Find BD.
4) If one angle is 90°, what are all angles?

step_by_step.explanation: All angles are 90°. The parallelogram is a rectangle.

summary: A parallelogram is defined by parallel opposite sides. Key properties: (1) Opposite sides are congruent. (2) Opposite angles are congruent. (3) Consecutive angles are supplementary. (4) Diagonals bisect each other. These properties are proved using triangle congruence and form the foundation for all special parallelograms.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with Parallelogram in center, branches for sides, angles, diagonals, and proofs.
review_questions_image.caption: Properties of Parallelograms Review.
